examples.title: File Locking Implementation using Semaphore
examples.description: Demonstrates how to implement shared and exclusive file locks using semaphores for concurrent access control
examples.code:
CLASS FileLock
  DECLARE sharedCount AS INTEGER = 0
  DECLARE mutex AS SEMAPHORE = 1
  DECLARE writeLock AS SEMAPHORE = 1
  
  FUNCTION acquireSharedLock()
    WAIT(mutex)
    sharedCount = sharedCount + 1
    IF sharedCount == 1 THEN
      WAIT(writeLock)
    END IF
    SIGNAL(mutex)
  END FUNCTION
  
  FUNCTION releaseSharedLock()
    WAIT(mutex)
    sharedCount = sharedCount - 1
    IF sharedCount == 0 THEN
      SIGNAL(writeLock)
    END IF
    SIGNAL(mutex)
  END FUNCTION
  
  FUNCTION acquireExclusiveLock()
    WAIT(writeLock)
  END FUNCTION
  
  FUNCTION releaseExclusiveLock()
    SIGNAL(writeLock)
  END FUNCTION
END CLASS
examples.explanation: This implementation uses two semaphores: mutex protects the sharedCount variable, and writeLock controls exclusive access. Multiple readers can acquire shared locks concurrently (first reader acquires writeLock, last reader releases it), while writers get exclusive access through writeLock.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for lock acquisition and release

examples.title: Python File Locking Example
examples.description: Demonstrates file locking in Python using fcntl module for advisory locking
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates advisory file locking using fcntl module. LOCK_SH provides shared (read) locks allowing multiple readers, while LOCK_EX provides exclusive (write) locks. The locks are automatically released when the file is closed or the process terminates.

examples.title: UNIX File Locking with fcntl
examples.description: C program demonstrating file locking using fcntl() system call for implementing readers-writers synchronization
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates using fcntl() for file locking in UNIX. F_SETLK attempts non-blocking lock acquisition, while F_SETLKW blocks until lock is available. F_RDLCK creates shared (read) locks, F_WRLCK creates exclusive (write) locks, and F_UNLCK releases locks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for lock operations (blocking version depends on contention)

examples.title: Deadlock-Free Lock Acquisition for Multiple Files
examples.description: Algorithm demonstrating how to acquire locks on multiple files without causing deadlock using ordered lock acquisition
examples.code:
FUNCTION acquireMultipleLocks(files, lockType)
  // files is a list of file handles sorted by some unique identifier
  SORT files BY fileId  // Always acquire locks in consistent order
  acquiredLocks = EMPTY LIST
  
  FOR EACH file IN files
    lock = createLock(file, lockType)
    success = acquireBlockingLock(file, lock)
    
    IF NOT success THEN
      // Release all previously acquired locks in reverse order
      FOR EACH acquired IN REVERSE(acquiredLocks)
        releaseLock(acquired.file, acquired.lock)
      END FOR
      RETURN FAILURE
    END IF
    
    APPEND (file, lock) TO acquiredLocks
  END FOR
  
  RETURN SUCCESS
END FUNCTION

// Example usage: Writing to two files atomically
PROCEDURE atomicTransfer(sourceFile, destFile, amount)
  files = [sourceFile, destFile]
  
  // Sort by file ID to prevent deadlock
  SORT files BY fileId
  
  result = acquireMultipleLocks(files, EXCLUSIVE_LOCK)
  
  IF result == SUCCESS THEN
    readBalance(sourceFile)
    readBalance(destFile)
    updateBalances(sourceFile, destFile, amount)
    releaseAllLocks(files)
  ELSE
    RETRY_AFTER_DELAY()
  END IF
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This algorithm prevents deadlocks when acquiring locks on multiple files by always acquiring locks in a consistent order (sorted by file ID). If any lock acquisition fails, all previously acquired locks are released in reverse order to prevent deadlock and allow retry.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting, where n is number of files
